layers.name: Gateway Layer
layers.description: HTTP/WebSocket server, routing, device authentication, protocol schemas

layers.name: Agents Layer
layers.description: Agent runtime, sandbox execution, tool system, skills, subagent management, auth profiles

layers.name: Plugin System Layer
layers.description: Core plugin runtime, contracts, and built-in plugins

layers.name: Plugin SDK Layer
layers.description: SDK and types for building OpenClaw plugins

layers.name: Channels Layer
layers.description: Messaging channel adapters (Telegram, WhatsApp, Discord, etc.) and normalization

tour.title: Project Entry Point & Gateway Boot
tour.description: Every OpenClaw deployment starts here. The boot module orchestrates the entire gateway startup sequence—loading config, initializing auth, wiring up channels, and spinning up the HTTP server. It is the single control-flow entry that coordinates all other layers, ensuring a consistent, predictable startup before any traffic is accepted.

tour.title: HTTP & WebSocket Server Infrastructure
tour.description: OpenClaw exposes a rich HTTP/WebSocket API built on a small, battle-tested networking layer. The net module sets up the raw TCP/HTTP server, while http-common and http-utils provide the request parsing, response shaping, and middleware composition used by every route. This is the transport layer that moves messages in and out of the gateway.

tour.title: Device Authentication & Rate Limiting
tour.description: Security lives at the edge of the gateway. The auth module implements device-level authentication, verifying tokens and session headers before any agent logic runs. auth-install-policy enforces per-device installation policies, and auth-rate-limit protects against abuse with per-client request throttling. Together they ensure only legitimate, policy-compliant devices can invoke agents.

tour.title: Agent Registry & Identity Management
tour.description: OpenClaw is a multi-agent platform, and the agent registry is the central catalog of all available agents. agent-list exposes the agent inventory to the gateway, agent-scope defines the permission boundaries each agent operates within, and assistant-identity manages the persona and capabilities of each assistant instance. These three files form the agent identity layer.

tour.title: Model Providers & Auth Profiles
tour.description: OpenClaw can route agent requests to multiple LLM backends. auth-profiles manages named credential sets for Anthropic, OpenAI, Vertex AI, and other providers, while auth-health monitors API key validity and rotation status. auth-profiles/display handles the human-readable formatting of these profiles. This layer decouples model selection from credential management, making it easy to switch providers.

tour.title: Sandboxed Agent Execution
tour.description: When a message triggers an agent, execution happens inside a sandboxed environment. The attempt module manages a single execution attempt—its lifecycle, timeout handling, and error propagation. errors.ts provides the typed error taxonomy (timeouts, auth failures, model errors) that lets the gateway make intelligent retry or escalation decisions. This is where agent code actually runs, isolated from the host system.

tour.title: Plugin SDK — The Channel Contract
tour.description: The plugin SDK is what makes OpenClaw infinitely extensible. channel-contract defines the interface every channel plugin must implement, channel-actions provides the imperative API (send message, react, upload file) that plugins call at runtime, and acp-runtime bridges the ACP protocol between the gateway and external systems. agent-runtime gives plugins access to agent capabilities without coupling to the core. This is the stable, versioned API surface for all third-party integrations.

tour.title: Plugin System — Setup, Contracts & Policy Enforcement
tour.description: The plugin system brings channels to life. setup-wizard-helpers walks users through onboarding new channel plugins, contracts/suites defines the capability suites each plugin can declare (messaging, media, presence), and group-policy-warnings enforces safety guardrails on group/room interactions. These files handle the operational concerns of running plugins at scale—installation UX, capability negotiation, and policy enforcement.

tour.title: Channel Core — Types, Index & Command Routing
tour.description: The channel layer is the adapter tier that translates between OpenClaw's internal message model and each messaging platform's protocol. plugins/index.ts is the main entry point for the plugin-based channel system, plugins/types.ts defines the shared type system for channel events and payloads, and command-gating.ts enforces which slash commands are allowed per channel configuration. This is the translation layer between the gateway and the outside world.

tour.title: Channel Policies — Allowlists & Health Monitoring
tour.description: Beyond simple command gating, OpenClaw supports rich per-channel access policies. allow-from.ts and allowlist-match.ts implement allowlist-based access control (who can message, who can be mentioned), and channel-health-policy.ts monitors channel availability and applies configurable failure policies. These files provide the safety and reliability guarantees that make OpenClaw suitable for production multi-tenant deployments.

tour.title: CLI Backends & Server Method Router
tour.description: OpenClaw ships a CLI for developers and operators. claude-cli-runner.ts implements the local Claude CLI backend integration, cli-backends.ts resolves and manages the available CLI backends, and server-methods.ts exposes every gateway operation as a named RPC method over HTTP. Together they provide both a developer-friendly terminal interface and the programmatic control-plane API that the web UI and other tools rely on.

tour.title: Hooks, Session Management & Bootstrap Infrastructure
tour.description: The final layer provides the runtime plumbing that ties everything together. hooks.ts is the gateway's event hook system, allowing external services to react to message lifecycle events; session-utils.ts manages the creation, lookup, and cleanup of agent sessions; and bootstrap-hooks.ts registers the startup-time hooks that initialize caches, budgets, and file structures. This is the infrastructure layer that makes the system robust and extensible at runtime.
